coredata.dc:title: Engineering a highly conductive honeycomb network on carbon cloth-supported bimetallic sulfide nanorod arrays for flexible solid-state asymmetric supercapacitors with superior performance

coredata.dc:description: Bimetallic sulfides represent electrochemical energy storage materials with potentially ultrahigh capacitance but poor rate performance and cycling stability for practical applications. Herein, a highly conductive carbon honeycomb network was successively engineered by repeatedly coating novel graphitic crystallite nanomaterials (GCNs) on hydrothermally grown carbon cloth-supported NiCo2S4 (NCS) nanorod arrays. It was found that the usage of GCNs rather than graphene oxide and carbon quantum dots was essential to constructing such carbon honeycomb networks. This honeycomb carbon network resulted in a significant improvement in the capacitance, rate performance, and cycling stability of the carbon cloth-supported xGCNs@NCS electrode. The engineered 3GCNs@NCS material obtained by coating GCNs three times had an ultrahigh capacitance contribution of 95.15 %, a capacity of 2112 F g−1 at 1 A g−1, an excellent rate performance with a capacitance of 1801 F g−1 at 20 A g−1 and exceptional cycling stability with 89.6 % capacitance retention after 10,000 cycles at 10 A g−1. Therefore, the 3GCNs@NCS material can be directly used as a binder- and additive-free electrode. The flexible quasi-solid-state asymmetric supercapacitor assembled with 3GCNs@NCS and a commercially activated carbon delivered energy densities of 50.5 and 40.6 Wh kg−1 at high power densities of 807.7 and 11087 W kg−1, respectively, retaining a capacitance of > 90 %, even after cycling 20,000 times at 5 A g−1. This work demonstrates that conductive honeycomb network-modified NCS nanorod arrays have excellent potential as flexible solid-state supercapacitor electrodes with both high energy and power densities and ultralong lifetimes.

head.ce:title: Engineering a highly conductive honeycomb network on carbon cloth-supported bimetallic sulfide nanorod arrays for flexible solid-state asymmetric supercapacitors with superior performance

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Bimetallic sulfides represent electrochemical energy storage materials with potentially ultrahigh capacitance but poor rate performance and cycling stability for practical applications. Herein, a highly conductive carbon honeycomb network was successively engineered by repeatedly coating novel graphitic crystallite nanomaterials (GCNs) on hydrothermally grown carbon cloth-supported NiCo2S4 (NCS) nanorod arrays. It was found that the usage of GCNs rather than graphene oxide and carbon quantum dots was essential to constructing such carbon honeycomb networks. This honeycomb carbon network resulted in a significant improvement in the capacitance, rate performance, and cycling stability of the carbon cloth-supported xGCNs@NCS electrode. The engineered 3GCNs@NCS material obtained by coating GCNs three times had an ultrahigh capacitance contribution of 95.15 %, a capacity of 2112 F g−1 at 1 A g−1, an excellent rate performance with a capacitance of 1801 F g−1 at 20 A g−1 and exceptional cycling stability with 89.6 % capacitance retention after 10,000 cycles at 10 A g−1. Therefore, the 3GCNs@NCS material can be directly used as a binder- and additive-free electrode. The flexible quasi-solid-state asymmetric supercapacitor assembled with 3GCNs@NCS and a commercially activated carbon delivered energy densities of 50.5 and 40.6 Wh kg−1 at high power densities of 807.7 and 11087 W kg−1, respectively, retaining a capacitance of > 90 %, even after cycling 20,000 times at 5 A g−1. This work demonstrates that conductive honeycomb network-modified NCS nanorod arrays have excellent potential as flexible solid-state supercapacitor electrodes with both high energy and power densities and ultralong lifetimes.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Supercapacitors are electrochemical energy storage devices with the salient merits of high power density, long-cycling stability, a broad operating temperature range, and high safety due to the fast physical adsorption/desorption of electrolyte ions and/or fast surface redox reactions that occur at their electrode/electrolyte interface [1]. However, supercapacitors usually deliver a low energy density of typically <10 Wh kg−1, thereby largely limiting their practical applications. Considerable research has hence been devoted to exploiting high-capacity supercapacitor electrode materials to meet the urgent demand for high power and energy density [2]. To date, the main strategy for developing high-capacity charge storage materials has been to synthesize active materials that mainly contribute to significant pseudocapacitance due to redox reactions in the charge/discharge process [3], which is much higher than that of conventional carbon materials (∼300 F g−1). In contrast, these active materials usually involve two kinds of battery-type and capacitive charge storage because of the occurrence of redox reactions in the electrode. The former is a diffusion-controlled process whilst the latter is a surface-controlled process. The electrode materials with a higher battery-type charge storage percentage usually deliver a lower power density. Hence, active materials, such as supercapacitor electrodes, are required to exhibit a high capacitance contribution percentage [4]. Although nanostructured active materials increase the surface capacitive contribution because of the increased surface area, their capacitive contribution is typically <70 % [1,2]. Generally, for the properties of power and energy density, improving one often compromises the other. Therefore, it remains a challenge to develop high-quality supercapacitor electrode materials for practical applications [1].

Extensive studies have demonstrated that many transition metal compounds, including oxides and sulfides, have great potential to provide high pseudocapacitance [5–7]. Bimetallic compounds were found to have greater stability and subsequent sulfidation increased their electrical conductivity [8]. Accordingly, bimetallic sulfides, such as NiCo2S4 [9], CuCo2S4 [10], and ZnSnS3 [11], have attracted significant attention as emerging supercapacitor electrode materials. Prominently, NiCo2S4 is the most attractive bimetallic sulfide for supercapacitors because it possesses the highest theoretical capacitance of ∼2000 F g−1 [12] and a relatively high conductivity of 1.25 × 106 S m−1 [13].

However, NiCo2S4 exhibits poor rate performance and inferior cycling stability as the supercapacitor electrode, which hinders the demand to develop supercapacitors with high energy and power densities. Accordingly, many attempts have been made to improve the charge storage performance of NiCo2S4 electrode materials. Typically, NiCo2S4 nanoarrays have been engineered on current collectors, like carbon cloth [14], nickel foam [15], and carbon paper [16], thus increasing the specific capacitance owing to more active sites being exposed to the electrolyte solution. Furthermore, the nanostructuring of NiCo2S4 benefits its ion transport properties and capacitance contribution. Unfortunately, nanosized bimetallic sulfides still exhibit poor rate performance and even worse cycling stability due to the enhanced volumetric inflation during the charge/discharge process, the promoted dissolution in the electrolyte solution, and the fragility of the nanosized materials [17]. It is reported that NiCo2S4 nanowire arrays experience a 30 % decrease in the capacitance after 1500 charge/discharge cycles [14]. Furthermore, carbon materials, including carbon quantum dots [18], graphene oxide [19], carbon nanotubes [20,21], and biomass-derived carbon nanomaterials [22], have been used to modify nanosized NiCo2S4. For example, Shwetha et al [23]. composited functionalized multiwalled carbon nanotubes (f-MWCNT) with NiCo2S4(NCS), and found that the optimized NCS@f-MWCNT electrode was increased by more than 1.5 times in specific capacitance, with capacitance retention of 89 % at 10 A g−1. Panicker et al [24]. confined growth of NiCo2S4 on porous carbon self-repairing g-C3N4/rGO heterojunctions(pCRNCS) as a supercapacitor electrode material, this pCRNCS electrode had a specific capacitance of 1938 F g−1 at 2 A g−1. The matched assembled pCRNCS//AC device with an energy density of 66 Wh kg−1 at a power density of 751 W kg−1, and remaining 93.6 % cycling stability after 6000 cycles. Although the utilization of these carbon nanomaterials can enhance the electrochemical energy storage performance of NiCo2S4 including specific capacitance, rate performance, and cycling stability, the enhanced performance can’t satisfy the practical requirement of developing the high-quality supercapacitors with high energy density and power density. Therefore, the challenge remains in developing nanosized bimetallic sulfide as supercapacitor electrode materials with high specific capacitance contribution, excellent rate capability, and long cycle stability.

Herein, we use novel graphitic crystallite nanomaterials (GCNs), which are abundantly obtained from traditional carbon materials, such as wood/bamboo charcoals, activated carbon, and biomass-derived charcoals, by a selective oxidation approach [25–29], to modify carbon cloth-supported NiCo2S4 nanorod arrays by repeatedly coating them and then carrying out a heat treatment at 900 °C. It is found that the charge storage properties of the NiCo2S4 electrode are comprehensively improved and the capacitance contribution percentage, capacitance capacity, rate performance, and cycling stability are all superior to the previously reported results when a conductive carbon honeycomb network was engineered due to the repeatedly coating GCNs. Moreover, the improvement due to repeatedly coating GCNs is much higher than the cases by repeatedly coating carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and graphene oxide (GO). Outstandingly, the GCNs-modified NiCo2S4 loaded in the carbon cloth electrode material reached an ultrahigh capacitance contribution of 95.15 %, an extraordinary specific capacitance of 2112 F g−1 at a current density of 1 A g−1 and maintained a cycle life of 89.63 %, even after 10,000 charge–discharge cycles at 10 A g−1. The assembled flexible solid-state asymmetric supercapacitors of GCNs-modified NiCo2S4 and a commercially available activated carbon delivered energy densities of 50.5 and 40.6 Wh kg−1 at power densities of 808 and 11,087 W kg−1, respectively, exhibiting an excellent cycling stability of >90 %, even after 20,000 cycles. Accordingly, this work provided advanced electrode materials with high rate performance and excellent cycle stability, for the construction of high-performance supercapacitors with high power and energy density.

Fig. 1 (a) depicts the synthesis procedure of modifying the carbon cloth-supported NiCo2S4 (NCS) nanorod arrays by repeatedly coating GCNs (xGCNs@NCS, with x (x = 1–5) layers of GCNs). The first step is to hydrothermally synthesize Ni-Co bimetallic carbonate hydroxides on a degummed and cleaned carbon cloth, which was woven from polyacrylonitrile carbon fibers (Fig. S1(a-h)), using Ni(NO3)2 and Co(NO3)2 as the Ni and Co precursors in a urea solution at 120 °C. SEM observations revealed that the Ni-Co carbonate hydroxide was vertically grown in the form of nanowire arrays on each carbon fiber of the carbon cloth (Fig. S1(i-l)). XRD analysis (Fig. S2(a)) identified that the as-synthesized Ni-Co carbonate hydroxide was a compound of (Ni, Co)(CO3)0.5OH·0.11H2O. The second step is the sulfidation of the Ni-Co carbonate hydroxide, in which the (Ni, Co)(CO3)0.5OH·0.11H2O was typically vulcanized by an anion exchange reaction between thioacetamide and (Ni, Co)(CO3)0.5OH·0.11H2O in a hydrothermal condition [30]. In the process of sulfidation, the oxygen atoms of the Ni and Co carbonate hydroxides were displaced by the sulfur atoms, thereby swelling the nanowires into the nanorod (Fig. S1(q-t)) with a diameter of ∼80 nm (Fig. S2(b)). XRD analysis (Fig. S2(c)) verified that the vulcanized (Ni, Co)(CO3)0.5OH·0.11H2O was fully transformed into NCS. The Raman spectra of GCNs, NCS, and xGCNs@NCS are presented in Fig. S2(e). The Raman patterns spectra of GCNs and xGCNs@NCS exhibited two characteristic peaks at 1327 cm−1 and 1580 cm−1, which were attributed to the D band and G band of the graphitic domain, respectively. This indicates that the GCNs were successively coated in NiCo2S4 nanoarrays. In addition, a little blue shift in the D band was observed in the Raman patterns of xGCNs@NCS, indicative of the formation of hybridized coupling between GCNs and NCS [24]. The integral intensity ratio (ID/IG) of the D and G bands changed less, with a nearly negligible variation from 1.07 in GCNs to 1.04 in xGCNs@NCS, further confirming the graphitic domains that the GCNs bear in xGCNs@NCS. The bands at 238, 300, and 373 cm−1 were seen in the Raman pattern of NCS [13], which become weaker in xGCNs@NCS so that these bands were difficult to clearly discern especially in those of 4GCNs@NCS and 5GCNs@NCS. This weakened band intensity for xGCNs@NCS was reasonably considered to be caused by the coating of GCNs on NCS nanoarrays.

Intriguingly, it is found that the Ni-Co carbonate hydroxide nanowire arrays grow only on one face of the carbon cloth supporter rather than two faces. To be exact, it is the downward face of the carbon cloth suspended in the solution where the Ni-Co carbonate hydroxide nanowire arrays grow in a hydrothermal reaction process, as illustrated by the photographs in Fig. S1(i-l). On the upward face of the carbon cloth, only a small amount of deposit sediment or even nothing was observed, as confirmed by the photographs in Fig. S1(m-p). This phenomenon was verified by several experiments by regulating the position of the carbon cloth suspended in the solution, independent of the face of the carbon cloth. Certainly, the NCS nanorod arrays were observed only on the downward face of the carbon cloth that was suspended in the solution after the sulfidation and heat treatment of the Ni-Co carbonate hydroxide nanowire arrays, which are comparatively presented in Fig. S1(q-t) and (u-x). We propose that this phenomenon was caused by the accumulating rate of Ni-Co carbonate hydroxide precipitant that was progressively produced by the hydrolysis of Ni(NO3)2 and Co(NO3)2 in an alkaline urea solution. In the process of hydrothermal synthesis, most of the Ni-Co carbonate hydroxide precipitant produced from the whole solution over the carbon cloth was so rapidly accumulated that there was not sufficient time to form the crystal products on the upward face of the carbon cloth suspended in the solution. In such cases, only amorphous sediments were deposited on the upward face of the carbon cloth, which was easily washed away with water. In contrast, beneath the carbon cloth, only the Ni-Co carbonate hydroxide produced from the solution of directly contacted carbon cloth slowly grows in a crystal state on the downward face of the carbon cloth. This unexpected selection raised by this synthesis process here permits one to simply and easily engineer a binder-free and conductive additive-free flexible electrode spontaneously with the electrochemically active material on one side and nothing on another side as a highly conductive current collector.

The third step is the coating of GCNs on NCS nanorod arrays. Prior to coating, polyethyleneimine (PEI) was used in advance to decorate NCS nanorod arrays by immersing the NCS nanorod-grown carbon cloth with a PEI solution. The decorated PEI could endow NCS with positively charged surfaces by protonating amine groups of PEI [31]. As such, the PEI-immersed NCS strongly adhere GCNs nanosheets with a feature of numerous hydrophilic oxygen-containing functional groups, like hydroxyls and carboxyls [25–29], and thus remarkably negative-charged surfaces via electrostatic interactions and hydrogen bonds between PEI and GCNs. Fig. 1(b) schematically illustrates this coating mechanism. In this manner, GCNs were repeatedly coated on NCS. Finally, the GCNs-coated NCS was heat treated at 900 °C in a flow of nitrogen. The obtained materials were labeled as xGCNs@NCS, where x (x = 1–5) indicates the number of coatings of GCNs. Their XRD patterns are shown in Fig. S2(d). It is confirmed that the constituent of NCS was not changed in these materials during heat treatment at 900 °C. The diffraction peaks of NCS are distinct without the coating of GCNs, which is weakened as a consequence of the coated GCNs on the surface of NCS.

However, the surface morphology of NCS was apparently changed with the progressive coating of GCNs. The SEM images of 1GCNs@NCS showed that, compared with NCS not coated with GCNs (Fig. 2 (a and b)), a small amount of GCNs being coated led to an increase in the surface roughness of the vertically aligned nanorods with the appearance of some protrusion on the nanorod surface (Fig. 2(c and d)), revealing that GCNs could strongly adhere on the surface of the nanorods during coating. With an increase in the coated GCNs x of 1–5, the nanorods were gradually bridged by the coated GCNs. Consequently, a honeycomb network was visualized among the coated GCNs and the NCS nanorod arrays in 3GCNs@NCS, as shown in Fig. 2(e and f). Eventually, the nanorods in the obtained 5GCNs@NCS were compactly agglomerated due to the excessively coated GCNs (Fig. 2(g and h)). A TEM observation showed that the diameter of the single NCS nanorod in the vertically aligned arrays was increased from ∼80 nm for NCS without coating (Fig. 2(i)) to ∼100 nm for 3GCNs@NCS (Fig. 2(j)). A “hairy” material was clearly observed on the surface of nanorods (Fig. S3(a-c)). This evolution in the morphologies was confirmed by their change in the surface areas, which are determined by the nitrogen adsorption/desorption isotherms (Fig. S3(d)). With an increase in the coated GCNs, the surface area was gradually increased from 17.8 m2 g−1 for the NCS without coating GCNs to the highest value of 31.6 m2 g−1 for 3GCNs@NCS and then decreased to 20.8 m2 g−1 for 5GCNs@NCS (Table S1). These nitrogen adsorption/desorption isotherms demonstrated that the adsorbed quantity by 3GCNs@NCS in the relative pressure of >0.4 was much higher than the other materials, indicative of a developed mesopore structure (Fig. S3(e)). This further supports the SEM observation that a honeycomb network evolved in 3GCNs@NCS. It is noteworthy that a repeated coating approach is necessary to form such a honeycomb network. This structure can provide more active sites for electrochemical reactions and allow ions and electrons to fast transfer in the interface and the interior of the electrodes. As a result, the charge/discharge process is allowed to proceed rapidly and the redox reaction inside the electrodes is to happen in a highly reversible way. We explored in advance the effect of the concentration of the GCNs solution in the range of 1–10 mg mL−1 on the electrochemical properties of the obtained GCNs-coated NCS. It is found that 2 mg mL−1 is optimal. Moreover, coating GCNs by repeatedly using a lower concentration GCNs solution produces a much more remarkable promotion to the electrochemical properties of the material than by using a GCNs solution of a higher concentration.

Furthermore, the content and distribution of the Ni, Co, S, C, and N elements of 3GCNs@NCS were analyzed using energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS), as presented in Fig. 2(k). The results showed that the Ni, Co, S, C, and N elements were evenly distributed on 3GCNs@NCS (Fig. S4). The atomic ratio of Ni:Co:S was 1:1.98:3.93, which was approximate to the stoichiometric ratio of Ni, Co, and S in NCS, thereby confirming the chemical composition of NiCo2S4. The selected area electron diffraction pattern of 3GCNs@NCS (Fig. 2(l)) further proved the cubic crystal phase of NCS. The content of the C element reached 36.4 atom%, indicating that a considerable amount of GCNs were successively coated on NCS. 3GCNs@NCS has a higher content of N element, 5.86 %, which comes from the GCNs and PEI. The N element of GCNs was introduced in the neutralization of acidic GCNs suspension with ammonium hydroxide in the preparation process of GCNs. The introduction of N atoms benefits to improve the electric conductivity of the network, owing to its rich electron character. In brief, the highly active material of xGCNs@NCS with a developed mesopore and the supporter of carbon cloth can be integrated as an electrode without using any adhesives and conductive additives in the preparation process of NCS electrodes largely promoting the charge transfer and ion transport.

Accordingly, the as-synthesized xGCNs@NCS was directly used as a binder- and additive-free working electrode in a three-electrode system to evaluate the electrochemical performance of xGCNs@NCS materials by the methods of cyclic voltammetry, galvanostatic charge/discharge and impedance tests. Fig. 3 (a) shows the CV curves of the xGCNs@NCS electrodes at a scan rate of 10 mV s−1. These CV curves clearly exhibited one pair of well-defined redox peaks, which are attributed to the reversible redox processes of Co2+/Co3+ and Ni2+/Ni3+ based on the following reactions:[32]

Distinctly, the coated GCNs remarkably increase the current density of the work electrodes, indicative of a significant promotion to the electrochemical charge storage of NiCo2S4. Especially, the pair of redox peaks, which was considered to be raised by the Faradaic reactions of NCS forming NiSOH and CoSOH [32], were strongly promoted. Moreover, the repeatedly coating GCNs enhanced this increase of the current density up to three times. In addition, it was noted that the electric potential required for the Faradaic oxidation reaction side of the pair of redox peaks was apparently increased while that for the reduction reaction side was decreased, indicating that the coated GCNs prompted the Faradaic redox reactions of Co2+/Co3+ and Ni2+/Ni3+. After repeatedly coating GCNs three times, the peak potentials of the xGCNs@NCS electrodes were not changed with the increased coated GCNs. These results suggested that the 3GCNs@NCS electrode possesses the greatest electrochemical charge storage capacity.

Furthermore, the CV curves of the xGCNs@NCS electrodes at various scan rates ranging from 5 to 50 mV s−1 were examined to elucidate the properties of electric energy storage, as shown in Fig. 3(b) for 3GCNs@NCS and Fig. S5(a-e) for the others. The increase in the scan rates normally resulted in an enlargement of the CV curves for all electrodes. Compared to the other materials, the 3GCNs@NCS electrode in a high scan rate can maintain the CV curves with a similar profile. This implied that the redox reaction that occurs for 3GCNs@NCS is highly reversible. The b values of the various electrodes were calculated in terms of the CV tests in the various scan rates according to the electrochemical test method described in the Supporting Information (SI). The anodic and cathodic b values were obtained by the slope of the fitted straight-line equation in Fig. S6(a-f). The change in the b value as a function of the coated GCNs times x for xGCNs@NCS is presented in Fig. 3(c). The anodic and cathodic b values of NCS and xGCNs@NCS were all in the range of 0.5–1.0, indicating that the charge storage of these electrodes possesses both battery and capacitance behavior. The anodic and cathodic b values were both increased with the increased coated GCNs and reached the maximum of 0.81 and 0.75 when coating GCNs three times, respectively. However, when the GCNs layer > 3, the excessive thickness prolongs the ionic transport distance, which retards electrochemical kinetics. The diffusion and capacitance capacity for each electrode were then calculated by the methods described in SI, as shown in Tables S2–S7. As expected, the capacitance contribution was remarkably promoted by the scan rate while the diffusion contribution was depressed, resulting in only a little decrease in the total capacity. This demonstrated that the electrochemical behavior of xGCNs@NCS electrodes was a process of surface control at a high scan rate. It was noteworthy that the capacity of the capacitive contribution leveled off at a scan rate of 20 mV s−1, except for the samples without the coating of GCNs (Fig. 3(d) and S7(a-f)). This revealed that the increased scan rate of >20 mV s−1 produced a negligible promotion to capacitance contribution but an inhibition to the diffusion contribution of the battery behavior. As a result, the capacitance contribution to the total capacity was gradually increased with increasing scan rates ranging from 5–50 mV s−1. Fig. 3(e) shows the diffusion and capacitance contribution of all xGCNs@NCS electrodes at a scan rate of 50 mV s−1. The 3GCNs@NCS electrode had the highest capacitance contribution of as high as 95.15 %. This manifested that the 3GCNs@NCS electrode can be used as the supercapacitor electrode with surface controlling characteristics.

The galvanostatic charge/discharge (GCD) tests of the NCS and xGCNs@NCS electrodes were conducted in a current density range of 1–20 A g−1, as shown in Fig. S8(a-f). The GCD profile of the 3GCNs@NCS electrode is presented in Fig. 3(f). In these GCD profiles of the xGCNs@NCS electrodes, one could observe an apparent charge and discharge plateaus and nearly symmetrical triangular charge/discharge curves in the higher voltage range. This implied that these xGCNs@NCS electrodes produce both pseudo- and double-layer capacitance. The 3GCNs@NCS electrode exhibited the most remarkable symmetrical charge/discharge curves and triangles. Accordingly, the 3GCNs@NCS electrode had the highest capacitance, which was consistent with the results obtained by the CV test. The total specific capacitance was calculated in terms of the galvanostatic charge/discharge profiles according to the method described in SI and was plotted as a function of the current density (Fig. 3(g)). Clearly, the coating of GCNs on NCS largely improved the capacitance and rate performance of the electrodes. Moreover, the coating times of GCNs exerted a significant effect on them. The 3GCNs@NCS electrode reached the maximum in both capacitance and rate performance, which coincided with the situation displayed by the CV test results. Specifically, the 3GCNs@NCS electrode exhibited the highest specific capacitance of 2112 F g−1 at a current density of 1 A g−1, much higher than 1585 F g−1 for the NCS electrode and higher than the previously reported values of NCS electrodes (Table S8). More outstandingly, the 3GCNs@NCS electrode at a current density of 20 A g−1 had a capacitance of as high as 1801 F g−1 and exhibited the highest capacitance retention of 85.27 %, which was much higher than 981 F g−1 and 61.53 % for NCS, respectively. These results demonstrated that the 3GCNs@NCS electrode possesses ultrahigh capacitance and excellent rate performance. This revealed that the coated GCNs largely prompted the diffusion of electrolyte ions inside the electrodes. In addition, a long-term constant current charge/discharge test was conducted for up to 10,000 cycles at a high current density of 10 A g−1. The cycling result (Fig. 3(h)) showed that the 3GCNs@NCS electrode retained a capacitance of 95.7 % and 89.6 % after cycling 5000 and 10,000 times, respectively, which largely exceed the capacitance retentions of 54.6 % after 5000 cycles and 32.3 % after 10,000 cycles for NCS without the coating of GCNs. Importantly, SEM observations (Fig. S9(a and b)) showed that the carbon honeycomb network in 3GCNs@NCS was kept perfectly after 10,000 cycles, indicating that this network bears excellent structural stability. Fig. S9(c and d) showed the XRD pattern and XPS spectrum of the 10,000 times-cycled 3GCNs@NCS. Fig. S9(c) showed that the XRD pattern of 3GCNs@NCS was nearly unchanged after cycling 10,000 times, indicating that the NiCo2S4 phase has a high cycling stability. XPS analysis showed that the Ni:Co:S ratio of cycled 3GCNs@NCS before and after cycling 10,000 times was approximately 1:1.98:3.88 and 1:2.01:3.26 (Fig. S9(d)), respectively. It is seen that the Ni:Co ratio remains at about 1:2. However, the S content was a little decreased, which is possibly caused by an interaction of NiCo2S4 with electrolyte in the charge/discharge process leading to a small amount of S ions being replaced by OH− of the electrolytes. Therefore, XRD, SEM, and XPS analyses consistently demonstrated that the 3GCNs@NCS electrode has excellent charge–discharge cycling stability. Table S8 lists the cycle lifetime of the previously reported modified NCS. It was affirmed that the 3GCNs@NCS electrode possessed much higher cycle stability than the previously reported NCS electrode.

Fig. 3(i) presents the Nyquist plots of EIS analysis of the electrodes and an equivalent circuit model obtained by the EIS spectra analysis and the magnified Nyquist plots in the high-frequency domain were inserted. Evidently, in the high-frequency domain, the coated GCNs resulted in a decrease in the diameter and contour length of the semicircle until GCNs were coated three times, and then a slight variation as coating GCNs by four to five times. This implied that the coating of GCNs on NCS remarkably decreased the charge transfer resistance (Rct) of the electrode with the 3GCNs@NCS electrode being the lowest Rct. The Rct values (Table S9) obtained in terms of the diameter of the semicircle affirmed this change. The Rct was decreased from 0.634 Ω for NCS without coating GCNs to a very low value of 0.283 Ω for the 3GCNs@NCS electrode. This revealed that the coated GCNs remarkably enhanced the charge transfer of the electrodes with the 3GCNs@NCS being the fastest. Consequently, the reversible Ni2+/Ni3+ and Co2+/Co3+ redox reactions that take place in the 3GCNs@NCS electrode possess the fastest kinetics, endowing an excellent rate performance and the highest capacitance contribution. Furthermore, the distance from the initial position of the semicircles to the original point of the real axis Z’ was obviously reduced by the coated GCNs and then a little increase in the high-frequency domain of the EIS spectra. This distance determines the electric resistance (Rs) of the electrode, which depends on the intrinsic resistance of electroactive substances, the ionic resistance within the electrolyte, and the interfacial contact resistance. Table S9 shows that the Rs were obviously decreased by the coated GCNs from 0.710 Ω for NCS to the lowest value of 0.509 Ω for 3GCNs@NCS. This revealed that the coated GCNs of less than three times significantly reduced the intrinsic resistance of the electrode and the interfacial contact resistance. In the low-frequency domain, the slope of the straight line was remarkably augmented by the coated GCNs on NCS up to three times, indicative of a decrease in diffusion impendence. This manifested that the coated GCNs significantly prompted the ion diffusion inside the electrode.

In summary, the three methods of cyclic voltammetry, GCD, and AC impedance tests consistently demonstrated that the repeatedly coated GCNs significantly improve the comprehensive electrochemical properties, including the capacitance capacity, rate performance, and cycling stability of xGCNs@NCS electrodes for charge storage. Outstandingly, the 3GCNs@NCS electrode achieved the best performance in capacity, rate performance, and cycling stability, which are superior to the previously reported results of the NiCo2S4 electrode. When GCNs were repeatedly coated more than three times, the electrochemical properties were to some degree decreased. We considered that the comprehensively improved electrochemical properties are attributed to the reduced intrinsic resistance of the electrode, the significant promotion of ion diffusion, and the kinetics of reversible redox reactions inside the electrode due to the coated GCNs. This is because the coated GCNs remarkably change the surface morphology of xGCNs@NCS. As mentioned above, repeatedly coating the GCNs three times produced a honeycomb network structure on the surface of NCS with a higher surface area and developed mesopores. The following heat treatment at 900 °C endows these honeycomb GCNs with excellent electric conductivity. Consequently, such a honeycomb surface texture with a high electric conductivity and the developed mesopores significantly reduce the intrinsic resistance of the electrodes and accelerate the ion diffusion and charge transfer inside the electrode, owing to the considerable electrolyte solutions to be allowed to accommodate the mesopores inside the electrodes. Eventually, 3GCNs@NCS achieved an excellent capacitive characteristic with a high contribution of capacitance in the process of electrochemical energy storage.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Significance of conductive honeycomb carbon network
ce:section.ce:para: It was reported that the addition of CQDs and GO could improve the electrochemical properties of NCS [11,18,20]. Accordingly, we synthesized xCQDs@NCS and xGO@NCS by using the same approach of coating GCNs, as described in detail in the Experimental section of the SI. Likewise, the electrochemical properties of xCQDs@NCS and xGO@NCS were examined by the methods of cyclic voltammetry, GCD, and AC impedance tests. The results are presented in Figs. S10–S12 and Tables S10 and S11 for xCQDs@NCS, and Figs. S13–S15 and Tables S12 and S13 for xGO@NCS. Consistent with the previous reports, the coated CQDs and GO improve the electrochemical properties of NCS. Moreover, the number of CQD and GO coatings exerted a vital effect on the prompted electrochemical properties and 4CQDs@NCS and 2GO@NCS exhibited the maximum improvement in the series of xCQDs@NCS and xGO@NCS, respectively. Therefore, the CV curves, capacitance contribution, rate performance, cycling stability, and impedance of the 3GCNs@NCS, 4CQDs@NCS, and 2GO@NCS electrodes are systematically compared in Fig. 4 to evaluate the effect of the coated carbon nanomaterials.

Fig. 4(a) demonstrates that the coated GCNs produce the most marked improvement in the electrochemical properties of the NCS electrode compared to the coated CQDs and GO. The electrochemical energy storage properties, including the capacitance contribution and capacity, rate performance, and cycling stability of the 3GCNs@NCS electrode comprehensively outperform that of 4CQDs@NCS and 2GO@NCS (Fig. 4(a-d)). Specifically, the capacitive contributions of the 3GCNs@NCS, 4CQDs@NCS, and 2GO@NCS electrodes were 95.15 %, 84.19 %, and 69.33 % at a high scan rate of 50 mV s−1, respectively (Fig. 4(b) and S16(a-f)). The capacitance capacity of 3GCNs@NCS at 20 A g−1(1801 F g−1) was close to that of 4CQDs@NCS at 1 A g−1(1865 F g−1) and was much higher that of 2GO@NCS at 1 A g−1(1641 F g−1) (Fig. 4(c)). The capacitance retention was 89.63 %, 84.06 %, and 37.41 % for 3GCNs@NCS, 4CQDs@NCS, and 2GO@NCS after cycling 10,000 times (Fig. 4(d)), respectively. Nevertheless, the coated GO contributed to only a small improvement. The EIS spectra in the high-frequency domain verified that the 3GCNs@NCS electrode had a much lower resistance of Rs and Rct than the 4CQDs@NCS and 2GO@NCS electrodes (Fig. 4(e)), indicative of a much higher electric conductivity. A much larger slope of the line in the EIS spectra of the 3GCNs@NCS electrode in the low-frequency domain suggested that the 3GCNs@NCS electrode possessed a much faster ion transport ability than the other two. Furthermore, the imaginary capacitance (C''(ω)) of the capacitance and the impedance frequency f of the electrode was plotted in terms of the formula in SI, as presented in Fig. S17. In this diagram, the reciprocal of the frequency f at the peak represents the capacitance response time. Fig. S17 showed that 3GCNs@NCS had the largest value of the frequency at the peak, indicating the shortest capacitance response time among these modified NCS nanoarrays. This further confirmed that 3GCNs@NCS has minimal capacitance loss and superior rate performance [33]. Based on the EIS tests, the linear relationship between the Z’ value and ω−0.5 was obtained (Fig. 4(f) and Table S14) [34]. The diffusion coefficient of electrolyte ion OH− in the electrodes was calculated in terms of the slop of the line, which was 2.33 × 10−9, 3.34 × 10−10, 1.03 × 10−10 and 5.02 × 10−11 cm2 s−1for 3GCNs@NCS, 4CQDs@NCS, 2GO@NCS and NCS, respectively. Obviously, the ion diffusion coefficient in the 3GCNs@NCS electrode was as high as 7, 22.6, and 46 times that of 4CQDs@NCS, 2GO@NCS, and NCS, respectively.

A comparison of the electrochemical properties of 3GCNs@NCS, 4CQDs@NCS, 2GO@NCS, and NCS strongly supported that the coated GCNs greatly improved the comprehensive electrochemical properties of NCS including capacitance, rate performance, and cycling stability. For elucidating the most improved performance of 3GCNs@NCS electrode, SEM was also used to observe the evolution in the surface morphology in the case of coating CQDs or GO with the coating times, as shown in Fig. 5 . A comparison of SEM images of xGCNs@NCS, xCQDs@NCS, and xGO@NCS (Figs. 2 and 5) shows that the morphology evolution greatly differed with the coated carbon nanomaterials. Clearly, in the case of xCQDs@NCS and xGO@NCS, the honeycomb conductive carbon network formed in the case of xGCNs@NCS was difficult to view with an increase of the coated CQDs and GO. For xCQDs@NCS, Fig. 5(a-d) illustrate that the coated CQDs increased the diameter of the single NCS nanorod and were inclined to adhere the single nanorods together into an integrity (Fig. 5(e and f)) at a high value of x. For xGO@NCS, the coated GO was preferential to cover the whole surface of NCS nanoarrays rather than coat the single nanorods of the nanoarrays (Fig. 5(g and h)). As a result, at a high x value, an integrated and thicker GO membrane was clearly covered on the NCS nanorod arrays while the nanorods were discretely kept with a nearly negligible changed size (Fig. 5(i-l)). Their surface areas confirmed the SEM observation. The surface areas of 4CQDs@NCS and 2GO@NCS are 21.2 and 19.4 m2 g−1 (Fig. 6 (a)), respectively, which was a little higher than 17.8 m2 g−1 of NCS without coating any carbon nanomaterials but much less than 31.6 m2 g−1 of 3GCNs@NCS (Table S15). Undoubtedly, the coating of carbon nanomaterials produces different microstructures.

The discrepancy in the morphological evolution of xGCNs@NCS, xCQDs@NCS, and xGO@NCS was believed to be caused by the difference in the dimensions of GCNs, CQDs, and GO. SEM and TEM observation verified that GCNs are in the size of ∼10 nm, CQDs is ∼5 nm or less and GO is 20 nm or larger (Fig. S18(a-f)). In the process of coating, CQDs is preferentially adhered around the nanorods progressively thickening and ultimately aging the nanorods and eventually agglomerating the nanorod arrays. The GO of nanosheets with a larger dimension is liable to cover the tops of the nanorod arrays and hard to adhere on the surface of a single nanorod. In contrast, GCNs with a nanosheet of ∼10 nm is liable to connect the nanorods as a bridge. Our previous investigation found that the GCNs can easily evolve into a gel in a high-concentrated aqueous solution, leading to a well-developed pore structure [26,28,29]. Consequently, a higher dosage of the coated GCNs produces a honeycomb network among the nanorod arrays. Fig. 6(g) depicts this discrepancy in the microstructures of 3GCNs@NCS, 4CQD@NCS, and 2GO@NCS during coating.

The survey XPS analysis (Fig. 6(b) and Table S16), showed that the 3GCNs@NCS, 4CQDs@NCS, 2GO@NCS, and NCS have a similar Ni:Co:S atomic ratio of ∼1:1.98:3.88. This implies that the coated carbon nanomaterials have no impact on the stoichiometric ratio of the matrix NiCo2S4. The detection of C (33.78 at.%) and N (3.89 at.%) in 3GCNs@NCS indicated that GCNs were substantially coated on the NCS surface, supporting the EDS results. The XPS spectra of Ni 2p, Co 2p, S 2p and N 1s were refined using the Gaussian fitting method and described in the SI. The results verified that the Ni2+/Ni3+ and Co2+/Co3+ substantially coexist in the xGCNs@NCS, xCQDs@NCS and xGO@NCS, providing a considerable number of active sites for the electrochemical redox reactions. More importantly, it is noticeable that the binding energies of Ni 2p and Co 2p in the 3GCNs@NCS material were positively shifted into a larger value by ∼0.8 eV and ∼0.9 eV (Fig. 6(c, d), and Tables S17 and S18) but such a shift was nearly negligible for 4CQDs@NCS and 2GO@NCS compared to the case without coating carbon nanomaterials. This shift was caused by the N heteroatoms introduced due to the coating and subsequent heat treatment of GCNs, which is not detected in the case of coating CQDs and GO. These introduced nitrogen-containing groups have a trend to chemically combine the metal ions in the interface of NCS and GCNs due to nitrogen atoms of a higher electron density [35,36]. This further supports the finding that the coated GCNs remarkably decrease the Rs and Rct of the electrodes. Additionally, the Ni 2p and Co 2p XPS spectra showed that the Ni2+:Ni3+ and Co2+:Co3+ ratio in 3GCNs@NCS were 0.40:1 and 0.38:1, higher than 0.11:1 and 0.24:1 in NCS and the other two (Table S19). This is because the GCNs bear a relatively higher electron density and thus chemically adsorb the species of Co3+ and Ni3+ leading to lower-valence Co2+ and Ni2+. For S 2p XPS spectra, the percentage of Co-S and Ni-S linkages increased by 9.81 % and decreased by 6.64 % (Fig. 6(e) and Table S20), but they have little change in 4CQDs@NCS and 2GO@NCS compared to NCS (Table S19). In addition, the binding energy of N 1s in 3GCNs@NCS has a negative shift of ∼0.4 eV compared to the heat-treated GCNs (Fig. 6(f) and Table S21). These results obtained by XPS demonstrated that the coating and subsequent heat treatment of GCNs on NCS resulted in a chemical binding between the electron–defect sites of NCS and the electron-rich GCNs owing to their strong interaction. Such chemical binding favors the electron transfer and chemical stability of the GCNs-coated NCS, leading to a significant decrease in the internal resistance and an increase in the redox reaction rate of the electrode materials. Chemically, this strong coupling formed between the coated GCNs and NCS explains the fact that the 3GCNs@NCS electrode has an ultrahigh capacitance contribution and capacity, excellent rate performance and excellent cycling stability.

In summary, the results obtained from SEM, TEM and XPS analysis give an explicit and convincing explanation of the excellent electrochemical energy storage that 3GCNs@NCS possesses. In the morphology, a honeycomb network with developed mesopores evolved when repeatedly coating GCNs three times, which favors the fast transportation of electrolyte ions inside the electrode. In the chemical structure, the coated GCNs strongly interact with the matrix NCS forming some kind of chemical bonds at the interface of the coated GCNs and NCS. Such strong-coupling interfaces to be formed between metal compounds and nitrogen-containing carbon materials can serve as the pathway for efficient electron transport within the whole electrode [37,38], remarkably reducing the interfacial and internal resistance. In addition, the carbon honeycomb network natively possesses a high electric conductivity. Therefore, the highly conductive honeycomb carbon network engineered on NCS nanorod arrays plays a crucial role in significantly promoting the comprehensive charge storage performance of the 3GCNs@NCS electrode.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical performance of asymmetric flexible solid-state hybrid supercapacitor
ce:section.ce:para: A flexible asymmetric supercapacitor with a sandwich-like structure was constructed using the 3GCNs@NCS and a commercially available activated carbon (AC) as the positive and negative electrode, respectively, as presented in Fig. 7 (a). It is noteworthy that the 3GCNs@NCS is a binder- and additive-free electrode, in which the active materials are spontaneously grown in the downward face of the suspended carbon cloth in the solution while the raw other face was naturally used as the current collector. A quasi-solid-state PVA/KOH gel was used as the electrolyte. The surface area and micropore and mesopore volumes of the AC are 2418 m2 g−1, 0.687 and 0.465 cm3 g−1, respectively, which were calculated in terms of the nitrogen adsorption/desorption isotherm (Fig. S19(a, b) and Table S22). The mass of the positive and negative electrodes was optimized to equilibrate the charges of the two electrodes (Fig. S20(a)). A preliminary experiment showed that pronounced polarization fluctuations appeared in the CV curves of the 3GCNs@NCS//AC/CC device with an increase of voltage window up to 1.7 V (Fig. 7(b)). Hence, the CV test of the device was conducted in the voltage window of 0.0–1.6 V, as presented in Fig. 7(c). It was evident that the CV curves of the device had no obvious redox peaks and remained a similar shape even at a high scan rate of 100 mV s−1, revealing that the device has excellent high-rate charge/discharge characteristics. Moreover, the CV curves of the 3GCNs@NCS//AC/CC device (Fig. 7(d and e)) had no significant variation when the device was bent at an angle of 0–90°, suggesting that the constructed supercapacitor can withstand the bending action as a potential flexible device.

Fig. 7(f) shows the GCD profiles of the device at varied current densities. It was seen that these GCD curves appear in an approximately symmetrical triangular shape and no significant plateaus were observed in the charge/discharge process. This manifested that the Faradic redox reaction that occurred in the device was highly reversible and hence contributed to a high capacitance contribution and charge storage capacity. Meanwhile, the GCD curve exhibits a minor IR drop with R about 3.78 Ω at 1 A g−1, where I is the charging/discharging current value and R is the equivalent series resistance (ESR) of the system, indicative of a little capacitive loss in the device. The GCD results showed that the device delivered a specific capacitance of 142F·g−1 at a current density of 1 A g−1 and a high value of 120 F g−1 even at 20 A g−1 with a capacitance retention of 84.5 % (Table S23), indicating that this device had an excellent rate performance. Furthermore, the 3GCNs@NCS//AC/CC exhibited exceptional cyclic stability and maintained 90.21 % of the capacitance after cycling 20,000 times at 5 A g−1 (Fig. 7g). The EIS test verified that the 3GCNs@NCS//AC/CC possessed a low value of Rct (2.18 Ω) and Rs (1.91 Ω) (Fig. S19(b)), although a quasi-solid-state gel electrolyte was employed, and the sum of both almost coincided with the ESR. Table S24 lists the capacitance, rate performance, and cycling stability of the previously reported NCS device. It is seen that the 3GCNs@NCS//AC/CC device significantly outperforms the previous reports. Therefore, it is believed that the 3GCNs@NCS//AC/CC has excellent potential to be applied as a high-quality, flexible and quasi-solid electrolyte supercapacitor.

More importantly, the Ragone diagram of ASC showed that the 3GCNs@NCS//AC/CC device delivered an energy density of as high as 50.5 Wh kg−1 at a power density of 807.7 and 40.6 Wh kg−1 even at an ultrahigh power density of 11,087 W kg−1 (Fig. 7(h) and Table S25). The previously reported results on the bimetallic sulfide electrochemical device were added in Fig. 7(h), such as NiCo2S4/NCF//OMC/NCF (45.5 Wh kg−1 at 512 W kg−1) [39], NiCoMn–S-h//RGO (44.9 Wh kg−1 at 780 W kg−1) [40], PCF//NiCo2S4/CF(28.8 Wh kg−1 at 878.3 W kg−1) [41], NiCo2S4-rGO(16 %)//NCCF-rGO (36 Wh kg−1 at 1600 W kg−1) [42], EEG@NiCo2S4//NMCS(18.4 Wh kg−1 at 4014.5 W kg−1) [43], CBC-N@NiCo2S4//CBC-N(23.5 Wh kg−1 at 7500 W kg−1) [44], NiCo2S4//PC(26.56 Wh kg−1 at 8000 W kg−1) [45], NCSRC-24//N-RGO(33.67 Wh kg−1 at 8009.19 W kg−1) [46], NiCo2S4/CNT//Fe2O3/CNT(17.1 Wh kg−1 at 8900 W kg−1) [47] and NiCo2S4/CC//CNF(21.8 Wh kg−1 at 14,000 W kg−1) [48]. It is well known that supercapacitors own the outstanding merit of a high power density of ∼104 W kg−1 but an unsatisfactory energy density of commonly less than 10 Wh kg−1, which largely constrains their application [49]. Fig. 7(h) demonstrates that the 3GCNs@NCS//AC/CC device has high both energy and power density. As an example of application, two 3GCNs@NCS//AC/CC devices (size of 20×20 mm and weight of 200 mg) were connected to power low-voltage devices, as shown in Fig. S21. After being charged at a high current density of 10 A g−1 for only 10 s, the connected ASC devices successfully powered a round red LED (2.2 V and 20 mA) to continuously illuminate for more than 10 min or to continuously drive a miniature direct current motor (3.0 V and 50 mA) to spin at high speeds for almost 1 min (Fig. 7(i)). These findings sufficiently proved that the 3GCNs@NCS//AC/CC device has excellent potential to be applied as a high-quality flexible quasi-solid state supercapacitor in the field of energy storage.

Novel carbon nanomaterials of GCNs, CQDs and GO were used to decorate hydrothermally synthesized carbon cloth-suppored NCS nanorod arrays by a PEI-assisted and repeatedly coating approach, followed by a heat treatment at 900 °C. The CV and GCD tests showed that the coated GCNs and CQDs substantially improve the comprehensive electrochemical properties, including the capacitance, rate performance and cycling stability as the electrodes of supercapacitors. The coated GCNs bear the most remarkable improvement but GO produces only a small promotion. The SEM observations, EIS tests and XPS analysis demonstrated the significant promotion of the coated GCNs has mainly attributed to the engineering of a highly conductive honeycomb carbon network among the NCS nanorod arrays, which substantially improve the electric conductivity, charge transfer ability and ion transport of the electrodes. The 3GCNs@NCS electrode material exhibited an ultrahigh specific capacitance of 2112 F g−1 at a current density of 1 A g−1, excellent rate performance (capacitance of 1801 F g−1 at 20 A g−1) and exceptional cycling stability (retaining 89.63 % capacitance after 10,000 cycles at 10 A g−1), which are comprehensively superior to previously reported results. Interestingly, the hydrothermally grown NCS nanorod arrays invariably occur on the downward face of carbon cloth suspended in the solution but nothing is deposited on the upward face of the carbon cloth with a raw state. Correspondingly, the assembled flexible solid-state asymmetric supercapacitor 3GCNs@NCS//AC/CC delivered an energy density of 40.6 Wh kg−1 at a power density of 11,087 W·Kg−1 and maintained a capacitance of 90.21 % even after 20,000 cycles. This study highlights the assembled xGCNs@NCS can provide high energy and power density as the electrode of high-quality supercapacitors with excellent energy storage performance for a long lifetime.
